Scenario: Broad cosmic source + linear propagation effects
Horn result: Continuous source should appear in both horns
Interpretation: The time trace can look beam-shaped, but adjacent channels remain almost equally bright and a steady source repeats in the second horn.

Scenario: Intrinsically narrow cosmic transient + propagation effects
Horn result: Passes if it fades by >14.8 dB between horns
Interpretation: A narrow transient survives the path. Exponential fading requires tau < 50.4 s, or a switch-off in the available ~105 s timing window.

Scenario: 3-30 MHz OTH signals through linear sky/receiver path
Interpretation: Second- and third-order products from two HF tones top out at 90 MHz, far below 1420.726 MHz.

Scenario: Very-high-order harmonic from a single OTH/HF carrier
Interpretation: At least harmonic order 48 is required from a 30 MHz carrier; normal low-order receiver distortion does not do this.

Scenario: Two strong UHF tones create IM2/IM3 in nonlinear electronics
Horn result: Conditional on where nonlinearity occurs and horn imbalance
Interpretation: Low-order products can land exactly in-band. In the notional 100 K model, IIP3=-10 dBm requires about -47.9 dBm per tone at the nonlinear stage.

Scenario: Common-mode terrestrial RFI entering both horns
Horn result: Normally suppressed by differential horn switching
Verdict: Possible only with large signal plus asymmetry
Interpretation: At 1% horn/product mismatch, each horn would need a roughly 3050-sigma common-mode artifact to leave a 30.5-sigma difference.

Verdict: Rejected by one-horn result under ideal geometry
Interpretation: Narrow bandwidth alone is insufficient; a persistent fixed source should have generated the second horn response 172.37 seconds later.
